assumption_note: Standard Hass avocado "2-layer" flat is a well-established produce industry carton size at ~25 lb net -- the count/size grade in the name (e.g. "(48)", "(60)") describes how big the individual avocados are, not how much the flat weighs, so it's NOT a divisor: every 2-layer flat is treated as ~25 lb regardless of grade.

Standard citrus (navel/valencia orange) shipping carton runs ~37.5 lb net, regardless of the count/size grade in the name (e.g. "(88)", "(113)", "(138)") -- that code describes piece size, not case weight.
Standard grapefruit shipping carton runs slightly heavier than orange, ~40 lb net, regardless of the count/size grade.
Standard lemon shipping carton runs ~38 lb net, regardless of the count/size grade.
Standard lime shipping carton runs ~40 lb net, regardless of the count/size grade.

Standard US apple shipping carton is a well-established produce industry size at 40 lb net, regardless of the count/size grade in the name (e.g. "64-88 CT"). Real bug caught and fixed here: an earlier version divided 40 lb by the count, which is backwards -- a standard carton weighs ~the same no matter what size grade of apple is inside (that's the point of a standard carton) -- and additionally mis-parsed a "80/88ct" grade code (slash-separated) as a multiply-chain (80x88) instead of a size-grade pair, compounding into a $4,153/lb result for one row before the fix.
Standard Western pear (Bosc/D'Anjou/Bartlett) shipping box runs ~44 lb net, regardless of the count/size grade.
A standard broccolini bunch -- ~0.5 lb each.
English (seedless) cucumbers run larger/heavier than standard slicers -- ~0.75 lb each. Checked before the bare CUCUMBER pattern below.
Standard slicer cucumber -- ~0.66 lb (300g) each.
"-CT" here means pint containers per case, not individual cherry tomatoes -- a standard pint of cherry tomatoes runs ~10.5 oz.
A trimmed romaine heart (not a whole head) runs ~0.5 lb -- checked before the bare ROMAINE pattern below. Count often a chain (e.g. "12/3-CT" = 12 bags of 3 hearts = 36 hearts).
A whole (untrimmed) romaine head runs heavier than a trimmed heart -- ~1.0 lb.
Standard naan flatbread piece -- ~3 oz each. Count often a chain (e.g. "4/8-CT" = 4 packs of 8 pieces).
"-CT" here means pint/clamshell containers per case -- a standard blueberry pint/clamshell runs ~11 oz.
Standard globe eggplant -- ~0.75 lb each.
A "cobette" (mini corn cob) is much smaller than a full ear -- ~2.5 oz each.
"-CT" here means half-pint/clamshell containers per case -- a standard raspberry/blackberry half-pint runs ~6 oz.
A whole fresh pineapple runs roughly 2-4 lb -- used the midpoint, ~2.5 lb each.
A standard dairy half-pint container -- ~1.075 lb (dairy density ~8.6 lb/gal / 8 half-pints per gal).
A whole endive/frisee head -- ~0.5 lb each.
A standard kiwi fruit -- ~0.25 lb (4 oz) each.
A whole cantaloupe (9-count case = a mid-size melon) -- ~3.0 lb each.
A whole honeydew melon runs larger than cantaloupe (4-6 count case = a large melon) -- ~5.0 lb each.
A standard fresh herb bunch (cilantro, parsley, etc.) -- ~0.15 lb (2.4 oz) each.
A whole celery bunch -- ~1.5 lb.

Standard stone fruit (peach/nectarine) -- ~0.23 lb (3.7 oz) each, consistent with a ~25 lb carton at a ~108-count size grade. The count here is a bare number right before "CT" (e.g. "108-CT") -- an earlier hyphenated size-grade code in the same name (e.g. "72-SZ") is NOT part of the count and is correctly ignored since it isn't adjacent to "CT".
Standard plum -- ~0.233 lb (3.7 oz) each, consistent with a ~28 lb carton at a ~120-count size grade.
A whole lettuce head (green leaf, iceberg, etc.) -- ~0.75 lb each.
A petite/mini banana runs smaller than standard -- ~0.2 lb (3.2 oz) each.
A whole cauliflower head -- ~2.0 lb each.
A whole fennel bulb -- ~0.75 lb each.
A standard mango -- ~0.75 lb each.
A standard kale bunch -- ~0.75 lb.
